question: [File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user who gains write access to the shared /tmp filesystem touch /tmp/alive with a current timestamp to keep the liveness probe returning exit 0 even when the pinger process is frozen mid-transfer, preventing Kubernetes from restarting the pod and causing the operator account to be silently drained by a stuck retry loop?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user delete /tmp/alive to force the liveness probe to return exit 1, triggering repeated Kubernetes pod restarts, and because the pinger's submitWithRetry() re-executes on each startup without idempotency guarantees, cause duplicate HBAR transfers from the operator account on every restart cycle?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user create /tmp/ready prematurely (before the pinger has finished initializing its Hedera client) to make the readiness probe return exit 0, causing Kubernetes to route traffic and trigger transfer ticks before the client is properly configured, resulting in malformed or unintended fund transfers?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that os.Args[1] is consumed without length or content sanitization beyond the switch statement to pass a crafted argument that causes the process to exit with code 2 (default case), and if Kubernetes interprets exit code 2 differently from exit code 1 for liveness probes, suppress pod restarts while the pinger drains funds?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user manipulate the modification time of /tmp/alive (e.g., via touch -t or a bind-mount race) to a future timestamp so that time.Since(fi.ModTime()) returns a negative duration, bypassing the age > 2*time.Minute staleness check and keeping the liveness probe green indefinitely even when the pinger's heartbeat goroutine has stopped, allowing a stuck transfer loop to continue draining funds?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit a TOCTOU race between os.Stat('/tmp/alive') and the subsequent fi.ModTime() read by replacing /tmp/alive with a symlink to a file with a controlled mtime between the two calls, causing the liveness check to pass with a stale heartbeat and preventing pod restart while the pinger is in a broken transfer state?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user who shares the pod's /tmp tmpfs (e.g., via an init container or sidecar with shared emptyDir) continuously recreate /tmp/alive with a fresh mtime every <2 minutes to permanently suppress liveness-triggered restarts, keeping a pinger instance alive that is submitting transfers to an attacker-controlled destination account?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of any cryptographic integrity check on /tmp/alive to replace its contents with arbitrary data, and since os.Stat() only checks file existence and mtime (not content), keep the liveness probe passing while the pinger's heartbeat goroutine has been killed, allowing a zombie pinger to continue submitting transfers?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user cause a container clock skew (e.g., by exploiting a misconfigured time namespace or NTP manipulation) so that time.Since(fi.ModTime()) always returns a value less than 2*time.Minute regardless of actual heartbeat staleness, permanently bypassing the liveness staleness check and preventing pod restarts that would stop runaway fund transfers?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary reads /tmp/ready and /tmp/alive from a world-writable /tmp directory without any file ownership or permission validation (os.Stat() does not check uid/gid), allowing any process in the container to create these marker files and manipulate the probe outcome to control when fund transfers start or stop?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user delete /tmp/ready to force the readiness probe to return exit 1, removing the pinger pod from the Kubernetes endpoint slice, and then when the pod is re-added after /tmp/ready is recreated, exploit the gap in transfer submissions to cause a double-spend scenario where pending retries from the previous ready period overlap with new transfer ticks?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the 2-minute staleness window in the liveness check (age > 2*time.Minute) combined with the Kubernetes failureThreshold of 5 and periodSeconds of 10 to keep a pinger pod alive for up to 10 minutes after the heartbeat goroutine dies, during which the pinger continues submitting transfers from the operator account without any liveness enforcement?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the startupProbe configuration (failureThreshold: 120, periodSeconds: 1, using 'live' mode) by deleting /tmp/alive during startup to prevent the startup probe from ever passing, causing the pod to be killed before /tmp/ready is written, and then exploit the restart loop's retry logic to submit duplicate initial transfers on each startup attempt?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary does not validate that /tmp/alive is a regular file (os.Stat() follows symlinks) by replacing /tmp/alive with a symlink to /proc/1/status or another always-present file with a recent mtime, permanently satisfying the liveness check and preventing pod restarts even when the pinger's transfer goroutine is deadlocked?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the lack of atomic file operations when the pinger writes /tmp/alive (os.WriteFile with 0644 permissions) by racing to read the file during a partial write, causing os.Stat() to return an error (e.g., ENOENT during rename) that triggers a false liveness failure, causing an unnecessary pod restart and duplicate transfer submissions?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the world-readable permissions (0644) on /tmp/alive to read the RFC3339Nano timestamp written by the pinger's heartbeat goroutine, infer the exact timing of the pinger's internal tick cycle, and use this timing information to craft a front-running attack that submits competing Hedera transactions just before the pinger's scheduled transfer?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of a file lock on /tmp/alive to truncate the file to zero bytes between the pinger's heartbeat write and the healthcheck's os.Stat() call, causing os.Stat() to succeed (file exists) but with a potentially stale mtime from the previous write, leading to incorrect liveness assessment and uncontrolled transfer behavior?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user mount a read-only overlay on /tmp to prevent the pinger's heartbeat goroutine from updating /tmp/alive, causing the liveness probe to eventually fail after 2 minutes of staleness, triggering a pod restart loop that repeatedly re-submits the initial HBAR transfer on each startup, draining the operator account?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the default mode (no os.Args[1]) sets mode to 'ready' and checks /tmp/ready, by invoking the healthcheck binary with no arguments in a context where /tmp/ready exists, to obtain a false-positive readiness signal that causes Kubernetes to keep the pod in the ready state even after the pinger's transfer client has been invalidated?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that os.Exit(0) in the 'ready' case provides no information about the pinger's actual transfer health (only file existence), to keep a pod 'ready' while the pinger is silently failing all transfers due to an expired operator key, causing the operator account balance to be reported as healthy while funds are being lost to failed transaction fees?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the 15-second heartbeat interval in pinger/main.go combined with the 2-minute staleness window to create a timing window where the pinger's goroutine is blocked (e.g., in a long Hedera SDK call) for up to 1 minute 45 seconds without triggering a liveness failure, during which uncontrolled fund transfers accumulate?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary is a separate process from the pinger (not an in-process health endpoint) to create a split-brain scenario where the healthcheck reports 'live' while the pinger's main goroutine has panicked and recovered into a tight transfer retry loop, causing rapid fund drainage without triggering a pod restart?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of any rate-limiting or circuit-breaker logic in the healthcheck binary to invoke it thousands of times per second, causing excessive /tmp filesystem stat() syscalls that starve the pinger's heartbeat goroutine of I/O bandwidth, causing /tmp/alive to go stale and triggering false liveness failures that restart the pod and re-submit transfers?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary uses os.Stat() (which follows symlinks) rather than os.Lstat() to check /tmp/ready, by replacing /tmp/ready with a symlink to a directory, causing os.Stat() to succeed (directories have mtimes) and falsely reporting readiness when the pinger has not actually completed initialization?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the liveness probe uses the same /tmp/alive file as the startup probe (both use 'live' mode per values.yaml) to create a race condition during pod startup where a stale /tmp/alive from a previous pod instance (if /tmp is not properly cleaned between restarts) causes the startup probe to pass immediately, skipping the intended initialization delay and causing premature transfer submissions?
